Who had the most people on with them? Did you guys have people actually get on and watch with you? I had 34. Yeah? How many? 34. You had 149 views already. Geez. All right. 34, 100. Wow, that's amazing. 750. 750. That's proud of that. That's got to be anyone else's top on that? 750. One. One. Boom. Love it. You got it done. All right. Do you guys start to feel in the spots where it was really easy to tell the story? And then there's some spots where you kind of foul up a bit. That is literally the reason why I continue to podcast regularly. It's to practice storytelling abilities. So every single time I do any kind of episode or anything that I publish or put out there, it's always 60% story, 40% content. If I do any more content, if as long as it's mostly story heavy, the audience usually loves it. If it's the other way around, they're like, we want to know the tactical stuff. And I was like, hey, you always say that, but then I lose you. You know? So I was at the rapid in the story, every single thing that I do, and you'll start to find that also. And you'll find the stories that resonate with people. They'll come back and tell you about them. All right. We're going to get into, so that's kind of the basis. And we'll go back through and we'll create more of the secrets. We'll create more of the things you guys need in order to actually write the script. It's a race to the bottom. Who's willing to take the smallest margins the whole way? There is a, how many of you guys watch Studio C? You're seeing that. It's like Mormon Saturday Night Live. And he's the guy who keeps trying to get him to buy the stuff. And by one by one they fall as if they're getting shot taking his product. It's really funny. This is one of my favorite quotes by Joe Polish. And he says that there's no relationship between being good at something and getting paid. Okay. My dad is one of the best software architects I've ever, he's amazing. Have to reason your watch has worked the way to do when you push the button in your car to start it. Like he's brilliant. And he was an executive at IBM for a long time and very good at the thing. Right? But had a hard time doing what he wanted to income wise for a little while because he hasn't learned how to sell the thing. And all of you guys, a lot of times what happens with entrepreneurs is we get so stoked about the thing that we do that we forget that we're not in the business of doing the thing. We're in the business of selling the thing. That make sense? This is the second part of the quote. There is however a huge relationship between being good at marketing and getting paid. Okay. How many times have you got something in the internet? You bought something and you get it on your doorstep and it's like pure crap. Right? They did a great job selling you on the thing. You know, rather than just executing, executing, executing. So anyways, um, we're going to go to offer creation in order to get to offer creation. Um, we got to talk about ask campaigns. Who here has run an ask campaign before? They're fun. Aren't they? The first time I ever ran one, um, I was in college. I had been building funnels for people in WordPress. I had been doing all this stuff for a while. It's terrible. Do it anyway. Working with the different programs. There was this company that I was, I was building for and I realized that, um, I hated my customer, you know, and I talked about this beforehand, but, um, so I started as like, what, what really can I do with my customer? How can I get a different customer? So I changed my bait and I got it, ended up getting a different customer. And, and the first thing I did with them is like, okay, I want to prove to these people that, that, you know, that I can get results for them. So I said, Hey, I know you have no idea what a funnel is. Totally techno babble. I'm going to go build the whole thing for free. If it makes you money, let's talk about me getting paid. If it doesn't, don't worry about it. And that was my pitch. Boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. And I found like a good company who had a lot of customers. Someone I knew that could blow up. They had a mid tier product. I only need to make them a few extra sales a month to make them blow up. And I was like, this is the perfect storm. We're going to do this. And so what I did is I went out to, um, their existing customer base. Um, I think they had like 1500 people on their, on their list for a higher ticket product though. And I was like, cool. So I went and I wrote out to them like, Hey, what's the number one thing you guys are struggling with? And it was a health product. And these guys wrote back some of the most like tear jerking stuff I had ever heard. Like, Hey, I'm sitting next to my spouse. So I'm writing this who's dying in bed. We're wondering when it's going to happen soon. It's like, Oh, oh my gosh. And I had to read it in three different phases because emotionally it was hard to take all that in. And when I did that though, it did several things for me. I suddenly started realizing that they were all thinking one thing that wasn't true. They were all asking for these other products that we weren't offering. There's all these little cool patterns that started bubbling up. And when I did that, all I had to do was I created a very simple tripwire funnel, put their different products in that they, we weren't offering things like that and made them a ton of money right off the bat. And I was like, Holy crap, that's awesome. Like that's how this whole thing works. There's another one that we did this for. And I ran a business a few years ago. I hate cooking. I hate it so bad. Those are empanadas dressed up like wild west stuff. Anyway, we started going around. We were given one semester, I had one amazing marketing teacher. He's the CMO of Denny's and of Pizza Hut. He's the guy that invented stuffed crust pizza. I felt like we needed to, not worthy. Thank you for that. And all he would do is he would tell us, look, it doesn't matter what you think. It doesn't matter what you want. You're not filling your own wallet. You're not your target market. Get your own thoughts out of your head. Your own ideas don't count anymore. Done. You go and you ask all the other students what it is that they actually want. And the whole semester, all we had to do is just run a profitable business. We can't sell this type of thing on a webinar. And you shouldn't put yourself on the fulfillment typically of an actual stack. I'd like to. So, Russell mentions in one of his videos that one of his bonuses was a two-page script. He valued it at $500 or something like that. Sure. So, those types of things, the mental barrier is, how do I value something that's so small? Sure. That's such a high value. How do I want to buy value? I was running all the stuff on the side and I, Russell came in on a Saturday once. And I was like, and I was like, Hey, what's up? He's like, Hey, how you doing? I was like, I have requests from way more people than I could ever handle. And it's sucking my time up and none of them are paid. And they're all, you know, there was, there's a few people who were paid, but the rest of them weren't. And he goes, you know what is so funny about when people message you? I was like, what? He goes, here's the secret. I'm going to give it and change your life right here. You don't have to answer them. And I was like, what? That's so mean. And he goes, no, it's not. It's self preservation. And he's like, remember the principle of the guru on the mountain. Okay. We are all givers. We are so obsessed and excited over a thing. We want everyone to have it. We could talk everyone's ear off it. Tech no babble of crap at everyone. Right. We love our thing. But what ends up happening is your time is going to get, especially with this model. You're the guru on the mountain. They have to pay more to get closer to the guru on the mountain. Sat weirdly enough. You got to create barriers. You know, that's how this happens. So selling higher end coaching one-on-one with you, your time is worth a lot. Does that make sense? And I first started out and I was like, okay, $100 an hour. And I was like, no one's going to pay that way too many. I was like, oh, I got to change my customer because I don't like some of them. You know, $100 isn't high enough. $300 an hour, you know, $400, $500. And then that's like $1,000. You know, it's like, it's higher and higher and higher because it changes the customer. You get this three-core Funnels book and you get this and this and this, and it comes with a three-month trial of Funnel University. And so most of the time when we're selling some kind of continuity thing, which I recommend you all eventually get into, eventually, go and we package up so much free time of it as one of the bonuses in it. So click funnels. You get six months of it for free when you buy FunnelHacks. You know what I mean? That gives us six months to prove to you that it is the B's and these and train you how to use it so that when six months is up, you have a running business, the pain of disconnect is too high and you don't want to leave. So it's the same thing if you're doing an app, anything on a recurring basis like that, usually we'll package together at least the first couple of months and make that as a bonus thing. And even if it's the core thing you're actually wanting to sell, it's actually better to not sell it up front. What's wrong with that? It's not believable. Right. It's like you go into a used car salesman and you're like, he's like, hey, check this out. Sweet Ferrari, yours for four bucks. What's your first question? Like what's wrong with it? Why is it four dollars? What did the last guy do? Is someone dying it? Like what's going, yeah. So it's the same thing. So we typically go on a 10% ratio. If you're trying to sell a thousand dollar product, usually we try and assign the total value around ten thousand to eleven thousand dollars. And the way we assign the value comes back to, notice we're not saying retail. That's very key. That's not a retail value. That's value. And so what we do is we say, if you're having a hard time assigning value up front, think through in total how much it actually could bring that person in and that's what we're putting on. But if we were to get $1,000 upfront and this whole year, instead of trying to get a lot of people in at a drug trial, we probably would have had an extra $500 to $600. Absolutely. And what's nice about the $1,000 price point too is that it's just high enough that it's reachable for people emotionally depending on your selling. But it's also high enough that they feel like they've actually made a real investment on something. If you get like a $6 thing on Amazon, comes in and breaks you like, ah, whatever, $6. You know what I mean? So it's high enough that they actually have some believability on their side. It's kind of like with what we're doing here. I mean, sitting right here in this room is the best tools, the best training I know of. ClickFunnels is the best software. I don't know. This is the best of the best of the best. Right? I have to constantly tell Epiphany Bridge stories to help break and rebuild belief patterns of individuals who are sitting right here trying to figure out what it is that is possible. And that's totally it. For whatever reason, pricing high, if we price this event lower than $8,000, it brings in a person that has not solved the kind of problems in their life where they can go make a business. You know what I mean? And they turn into a project. And it's not to be rude, but that's how it happens. If we charge higher than $8,000 though, they come in. And for whatever reason, that's a weird price point, but that's what we've learned. And people who come in who've paid that level usually pay attention. And so that's why we do it that way. It'll come to you in the form of complaints. I wish your product did this. Wish your product did that. There was a product that I was selling and I was really proud of it. And it took me eight months to build and I put this thing together. I funnel hacked real deep on it. I went and it was a great product. Still is fantastic. And I was like, okay, I'm going to sell it in a cool tripwire funnel and I put it out there. And like nobody bought it. I was like, do you guys not understand what's going on? And like the three people went through said, oh, I love the product. But like, why can't you just give me the upsell for free? And I was like, because if you pay up here, turn it off, I get out of here. And I was mad. And my wife was like, what's wrong with you? I was like, these people are so freaking ungrateful. And I was like, fine, fine, I'll go and do it. So I added in the upsell as part of the original offer. Huge spike in sales. Okay, it helped go against the biggest false belief or tenure they had. And then we're going on, we're going on, we're going on. And same thing. And they wanted the next one. And I was like, what is going on? This is so ridiculous. And I was like, are these people just complaining? Are they freeloaders? What's going on? And finally I realized like, I had not done a good enough job creating a stack out of that original offer. And when I started putting in those upsells, and I started putting in those, all those, all those other things, and then that's what I offered, it was insane. It blew up that tons of, I mean, it does really well. And that was one of the first products ever launched on my own that just like exploded. And it was because of this principle.
